title: Process and apparatus for producing controlled atmosphere
abstract: 1511973 Controlled gas atmospheres S Z VASILIEV I I MAERGOIZ L A MIKHAILOV and L I PUSHKAREV 1 July 1976 [1 July 1975 3 July 1975] 27541/76 Heading C5E Controlled gas atmospheres are produced by supplying a starting product on to a catalyst heated externally by the combustion products of a hydrocarbon gas, the starting product being a mixture of an additionally fed hydrocarbon gas and at least a portion of the combustion products of the hydrocarbon gas employed for heating the catalyst. The process may be conducted in an apparatus of the type shown in Fig. 1 which comprises an airtight lined casing 1 provided with a wall-mounted burner 2 for combustion of a hydrocarbon gas. Preparation of an air-gas mixture is effected in a gas-mixing unit including an air filter 3, an air flow meter 4, a hydrocarbon gas flow meter 5, a controller 6 for adjusting the ratios between the air and hydrocarbon gas flow rates and valves 7 and 8 setting these ratios. Air and hydrocarbon gas are supplied in the directions indicated by arrows A and B respectively. Upstream of the burner 2 there are successively mounted along the air-gas mixture path, a gas blower 9, a control valve 10 and a fire screen 11. The hydrocarbon gas combustion products are removed from the pipe 12 in the direction of arrow C. The catalyst 14 is contained in bucket 13 which bucket has a perforated bottom 15 connected with an outlet pipe 16 for the obtained controlled atmosphere. Inlet pipe 18 is for supplying the hydrocarbon gas on to the catalyst in the direction of the arrow D. A cooler 19 for the controlled atmosphere communicates with outlet pipe 16, the resulting controlled atmosphere being evacuated in the direction of arrow E. Thermocouple 20 is provided in the heating space 17 to measure the process temperature. The controlled atmospheres are of particular use in the heat treatment of metals and alloys.
description:
(54) PROCESS AND APPARATUS FOR PRODUCING
CONTROLLED ATMOSPHERES
(71) We, SERGEI ZINOVIEVICH
VASILIEV, of kvartira 25, korpus 2, 12 ulitsa B. Ugreshskaya, Moscow, IOSIF
IZRAILEVICH MAERGOIZ, of kvartira 7, 12 pereulok Vasnetsova, Moscow, LEONID
ALEXANDROVICH MIKHAILOV, of kvartira 64, korpus 2, 12 ulitsa B.
Ugreshskaya, Moscow, and LEONID
IVANOVICH PUSHKAREV, of kvartira 7, korpus 1, 7 proezd Schelkovsky,
Moscow, all Union of Soviet Socialist
Republics, all citizens of the Union of Soviet
Socialist Republics, do hereby declare the invention, for which we pray that a patent may be granted to us, and the method by which it is to be performed, to be particularly described in and by the following statement:
This invention relates to a process and apparatus for producing controlled atmospheres, in particular for the heat-treatment of metals and alloys.
The present invention in one aspect provides a process for producing controlled atmospheres by supplying a starting product onto a catalyst externally heated by the combustion products of a hydrocarbon gas, the starting product being a mixture of an additionally fed hydrocarbon gas and at least a portion of the combustion products of the said hydrocarbon gas employed for heating the catalyst.
The mixture of the additionally fed hydrocarbon gas and at least a portion of the hydrocarbon gas combustion products employed for the formation of the starting product may be suitably taken in a volume ratio between the components of
hydrocarbon gas 1-30
combustion products 99-70
The hydrocarbon gas combustion products employed for the catalyst heating are preferably obtained at an air supply rate factor of from 0.6 to 1.05.
The portion of the hydrocarbon gas combustion products employed for the formation of the starting product preferably constitutes 50 to 100 vol.% of the total amount of the combustion products of the hydrocarbon gas employed for the catalyst heating.
The invention in another aspect provides apparatus for producing controlled atmospheres, comprising an airtight casing accommodating at least one vessel filled with a catalyst onto which the starting product is in use supplied, the casing having an outlet pipe for the obtained controlled atmosphere and at least one wall-mounted burner for combustion of a hydrocarbon gas and further having respective pipes for the removal of the hydrocarbon gas combustion products and for the supply of a hydrocarbon gas into the catalyst, the end portion of the said pipe for the supply of the hydrocarbon gas being positioned in the vessel filled with the catalyst, and the vessel filled with the catalyst being in the form of a bucket with a perforated bottom, the bucket communicating with the said outlet pipe for the obtained controlled atmosphere and having its open and extending into the heating space of the casing.
The end portion of the pipe for the supply of the hydrocarbon gas onto the catalyst may be inserted into the catalyst bed.
The pipe for the supply of the hydrocarbon gas onto the catalyst may be suitably disposed coaxially with the catalyst-filled vessel.
The pipe for the removal of the hydrocarbon gas combustion products may be positioned inside the catalyst-filled vessel and coaxially therewith.
The apparatus is preferably further provided with a cooler for cooling the removed combustion products of the hydrocarbon gas, the cooler being connected by means of a piping with the pipe for the removal of the combustion products.
The apparatus is preferably also provided with a ílow-rate controller for the removed combustion products of the hydrocarbon gas, the controller being connected by means of a piping with the cooler for cooling the removed combustion products.
The apparatus may suitably further comprise a controller for adjusting the flow rate of the hydrocarbon gas supplied onto the catalyst depending on the obtained controlled atmosphere composition, the controller being connected by means of a piping with the pipe for the supply of the hydrocarbon gas onto the catalyst.
The process according to the present invention for producing controlled atmospheres makes it possible to produce endothermal and exothermal controlled atmospheres of any composition within the range of variation of the air supply rate factor of from 0.25 to 0.95. The quality of the controlled atmospheres is also improved.
This is achieved owing to the use, as the starting product, of a mixture of the additionally fed hydrocarbon gas and at least a portion of the combustion products of the hydrocarbon gas employed for catalyst heating. Moreover, the process according to the present invention makes it possible to produce a controlled atmosphere with combustion of hydrocarbon gases at an air supply rate factor of from 0.4 to 0.6.
The apparatus according to the present invention for carrying out the above process for producing controlled atmospheres makes it possible to obtain controlled atmospheres of a widely varied composition in a single apparatus. The controlled atmosphere composition may be automatically adjusted by one of the atmosphere components. Creation of a controlled atmosphere in the apparatus according to the present invention upon combustion of hydrocarbon gases with an air supply rate factor of from 0.4 to 0.6 is effected without any external heat supply to the catalyst, whereby consumption of the hydrocarbon fuel can be substantially reduced.
The process according to the present invention for producing controlled atmospheres may be suitably carried out in the following manner.
The starting hydrocarbon gas in a mixture with air is supplied into a burner and burnt in a heating space at an air supply rate factor of from 0.6 to 1.05. The resulting combustion products with a temperature of from 900 to 1,600"C are passed into a nickel catalyst bed to which is additionally fed a hydrocarbon gas. The mixture of the additionally fed hydrocarbon gas and at least a portion of the hydrocarbon gas combustion products employed for the formation of the starting product is taken in the following volume ratio between the components:
hydrocarbon gas 1-30
combustion products 99-70
The ratio between the additionally fed hydrocarbon gas and at least a portion of the hydrocarbon gas combustion products depends on the required composition of the final controlled atmosphere.
The conversion products leaving the catalyst bed are cooled in a water cooler, wheafter they can be used as the final controlled atmosphere.
At an air supply rate factor of from 0.4 to 0.95 the process for producing controlled atmospheres is autothermal, i.e. the heat evolved by the combustion products is sufficient for heating the catalyst, thus ensuring normal progress of the conversion reaction between the hydrocarbon gas and the combustion products.
At an air supply rate factor of from 0.25 to 0.4, the mixture of the hydrocarbon gas and air is burnt in an amount which exceeds that required for the production of a controlled atmosphere. For this reason, a portion of the combustion products is removed from the heating space by-passing the catalyst; in doing so, the portion of the hydrocarbon gas combustion products employed for the formation of the starting product constitutes at least 50 vol.% of the total amount of the hydrocarbon gas combustion products employed for the catalyst heating.
For better utilization of the heat of the combustion products the catalyst is additionally heated by at least a portion of the hydrocarbon gas combustion products remaining after the starting product formation and constituting up to 50 viol.%' of the total amount of the combustion products of the hydrocarbon gas employed for the catalyst heating.
The invention will be further described, by way of example only, with reference to the accompanying drawings, wherein:
Figure 1 is a general schematic diagram of an apparatus for producing controlled atmospheres by the process according to the present invention, with parts of the apparatus being shown in sectional elevation;
Figure 2 is a view similar to Figure 1 of an embodiment of the apparatus for producing controlled atmospheres, with an inlet pipe for supplying a hydrocarbon gas inserted into the catalyst bed;
Figure 3 is a view similar to Figure 1 of an embodiment of the apparatus for producing controlled atmospheres, with two catalyst-filled vessels and two burners for combustion of the hydrocarbon gas;;
Figure 4 is a view similar to Figure 1 of an embodiment of the apparatus for producing controlled atmospheres, with an outlet pipe for the removal of the hydrocarbon gas combustion products disposed inside the catalyst-filled vessel;
Figure 5 is a view similar to Figure 1 of an embodiment of the apparatus for producing controlled atmospheres, with a controller of the hydrocarbon gas flow rate depending on the composition of the resulting controlled atmosphere; and
Figure 6 is a view similar to Figure 1 of an embodiment of the apparatus for producing controlled atmospheres, with a cooler for cooling the removed combustion products of the hydrocarbon gas and a controller of the flow rate thereof.
The apparatus shown in Figure 1 comprises an airtight lined casing 1 provided with a wall-mounted burner 2 for combustion of a hydrocarbon gas (a admixture of hydrocarbon gas with air, i.e. an air-gas mixture).
Preparation of the air-gas mixture is effected in a gas-mixing unit including an air filter 3, an air flow meter 4, a hydrocarbon gas flow meter 5, a controller 6 for adjusting the ratios between the air and hydrocarbon gas flow rates and valves 7 and 8 setting these ratios, all these parts being interconnected through piping. Air and the hydrocarbon gas are supplied in the directions indicated by arrows A and B respectively.
Upstream of the burner 2 there are successively mounted, along the air-gas mixture path, a gas blower 9, a control valve 10 and a fire screen 11, all these parts being interconnected by piping.
A pipe 12 for removing the hydrocarbon gas combustion products is mounted in one of the walls of the casing 1 in the upper portion thereof. The combustion products are removed from the pipe 12 in the direction of arrow C.
The casing 1 accommodates a vessel 13 in the form of a bucket (hereinafter referred to as bucket 13) filled with a catalyst 14 onto which the starting product is fed, the catalyst being heated by the hydrocarbon gas combustion products. A bottom 15 of the bucket 13 is perforated and connected with an outlet pipe 16 for the obtained controlled atmosphere. The open end of the bucket 13 extends into a heating space 17 of the casing 1.
Mounted in one of the walls of the casing 1 in the upper portion thereof is an inlet pipe 18 for supplying the hydrocarbon gas onto the catalyst 14, the end portion of the inlet pipe 18 being positioned inside the bucket 13. Admission of the hydrocarbon gas is effected in the direction of arrow
D. The inlet pipe 18 for supplying the hydrocarbon gas is arranged coaxially with the bucket 13 filled with the catalyst 14.
The starting product supplied onto the catalyst 14 comprises a mixture of the hydrocarbon gas fed in the direction of arrow D and at least a portion of the hydrocarbon gas combustion products employed for heating the catalyst 14.
A cooler 19 for the controlled atmosphere communicates with the outlet pipe 16 via a piping. The resulting controlled atmosphere is evacuated in the direction of arrow E.
To measure the process temperature during the production of controlled atmospheres, a thermocouple 20 is provided in the heating space 17.
The apparatus shown in Figure 2 is provided with an inlet pipe for supplying the hydrocarbon gas inserted into the catalyst bed 14.
It is advantageous to employ an apparatus as shown in Figure 3 having several (e.g.
two) catalyst-filled vessels and several (e.g.
two) burners for combustion of the hydrocarbon gas. In this case, in the walls of the casing 1 there are mounted two burners 2 and 2' for combustion of the hydrocarbon gas, the former being positioned in the lower section and the latter in the upper section of the casing 1. Admission of the air-gas mixture into each of the burners 2 and 2' via two lines accommodating control valves 10 and 10' and fire screens 11 and 11' is effected by means of a single gas blower 9.
Distribution of the air-gas mixture in two streams is effected by adjusting the control valves 10 and 10'. Inside the casing 1, there are two vessels 13 and 13' in the form of buckets (hereinafter referred to as buckets 13 and 13') filled with the catalyst 14 and 14' respectively. Mounted in the upper section of the casing 1 are two inlet pipes 18 and 18' for supplying the hydrocarbon gas onto the catalysts 14 and 14'. The inlet pipes 18 and 18' for supplying the hydrocarbon gas are disposed coaxially with the buckets 13 and 13' filled with the catalyst 14 and 14'. The perforated bottom 15 and 15' of the bucket 13 or 13' communicates via a piping with the outlet pipe 16 and 16' for the controlled atmosphere which in turn is connected by means of pipings with the cooler 19 and 19'.At the outlets of the coolers 19 and 19', control valves 21 and 21' are provided for distribution of two streams of the controlled atmospheres.
The apparatus shown in Figure 4 has an outlet pipe 22 for the removal of the hydrocarbon gas combustion products disposed inside a vessel 23 (hereinafter referred to as bucket 23) filled with the catalyst 14 and arranged coaxially with the bucket 23. In this embodiment, the catalyst 14 is located between the walls of the bucket 23 and the outlet pipe 22, whereby an annular vessel is formed.
This arrangement, wherein the pipe 22 is positioned inside the bucket 23, ensures additional heating of the catalyst 14 with at least a portion of the hydrocarbon gas combustion products remaining after the starting product formation.
In this case, a pipe 24 for evacuating the controlled atmosphere is also connected with the perforated bottom of the bucket 23, while an inlet pipe 25 for the supply of the hydrocarbon gas onto the catalyst 14 is somewhat offset with respect to the axis of the bucket 23.
In the apparatus shown in Figure 5 there is provided a controller for adjusting the flow rate of the hydrocarbon gas supplied onto the catalyst 14 via the inlet pipe 18, depending on the obtained controlled atmosphere composition, this controller being connected with the inlet pipe for supplying the hydrocarbon gas onto the catalyst 14 by means of a piping. This controller of the hydrocarbon gas flow rate comprises a valve 26 for rough adjustment of the hydrocarbon gas supply onto the catalyst 14, a control valve 27 connected through a by-pass line with the inlet and outlet pipes of the valve 26 for rough adjustment of the hydrocarbon gas supply onto the catalyst 14 and kinematically associated with a conventional actuator 28, and a flow meter 29 of the hydrocarbon gas supply onto the catalyst 14, all these parts being interconnected by piping.
The apparatus also includes a gas analyzer 30 for the analysis of the final controlled atmosphere composition, the analyzer 30 being pneumatically associated with the actuator 28.
In the apparatus shown in Figure 6 there is provided a cooler 31 for cooling the removed combustion products of the hydrocarbon gas the cooler 31 being connected by means of a piping with the outlet pipe 12 for the combustion products. The apparatus is also provided with a controller 32 for adjusting the flow rate of the removed combustion products of the hydrocarbon gas, the controller 32 being connected by means of a piping with the cooler 31. The controller 32 maintains constant the flow rate of the removed combustion products, while the amount thereof is controlled by means of a valve 33.
In the apparatus shown in Figure 4, the end portion of the inlet pipe 25 for the hydrocarbon gas supply is connected with a manifold 34 for supplying the hydrocarbon gas onto the catalyst 14.
As can be seen in each of the embodiments shown in Figures 1 to 6, the casing 1 is supported on a base 35 secured to a frame 36. The inlet pipes 18 (Figures 1, 2, 3, 5 and 6) and 25 (Figure 4) for supplying the hydrocarbon gas onto the catalyst 14 are mounted on the upper part of the casing 1 by means of a tightly sealed flange 37 (the inlet pipe 18' shown in Figure 3
is mounted by means of the flange 37'). The
vessel 13 (13') is provided, in the lower part thereof, with a pipe 38 (38') which is an extension of the vessel 13. Inside the pipe
38 (38') there is a ceramic heat insulation
39 (39') and the pipe end is provided with a cover 40 (40'). The pipe 38 (38') is secured to the casing 1 by means of a tightly sealed flange 41 (41').
The embodiments of apparatus for pro
ducing controlled atmospheres as shown in each of Figures 1 to 6 are each operated
in much the same manner. A detailed description of the operation of the apparatus shown in Figure 6 is given below.
The hydrocarbon gas is passed through the gas flow meter 5 and further into the controller 6 for adjusting the ratios of the gas and air flow rates. Air (through the filter
3, air flow meter 4 and valve 8) and the hydrocarbon gas (through the valve 7) are
delivered by the gas blower 9 into the burner 2, after having passed through the control valve 10 and fire screen 11. The air-gas mixture is burnt in the heating space
of the lined airtight casing 1 at an air
supply rate factor of from 0.6 to 1.05. The resulting combustion products at a temperature of from 600 to 1,600"C are fed from the top of the casing into the catalyst bed 14 placed in the bucket 13.The hydrocarbon gas is passed via a piping through the valve 26 for rough adjustment of the hydrocarbon gas supply onto the catalyst 14 and through the flow meter 29, and then into the inlet pipe 18 for supplying the hydrocarbon gas onto the catalyst; the hydrocarbon gas is fed onto the catalyst 14 by means of the inlet pipe 18, simultaneously with the supply of the combustion products, in the volume ratio:
hydrocarbon gas 1-30
=~~
combustion products 99-70
Conversion of the hydrocarbon gas with the combustion products occurs in the catalyst bed 14. The conversion products leaving the outlet pipe 16 are cooled in the cooler 19 and, downstream of the valve 21, they constitute the desired controlled atmosphere.
Analysis of the final controlled atmosphere composition is effected by means of the gas analyzer 30 pneumatically connected with the actuator 28 operating the control valve 27.
The predetermined composition of the controlled atmosphere is maintained constant automatically by varying the hydrocarbon gas supply rate with the aid of the control valve 27. With an air supply rate factor of from 0.4 to 0.95, the process for the production of controlled atmospheres is effected autothermaly, i.e. the heat of the combustion products is sufficient to heat the catalyst
14, thus ensuring the conversion reaction between the hydrocarbon gas and combustion
products. With an air supply rate factor of
from 0.25 to 0.4, the gas-air mixture is burnt in an amount exceeding that required for the controlled atmosphere production.For this reason, the portion of the hydrocarbon gas combustion products employed for the starting product formation constitutes 50 to 100% by volume of the total amount of the hydrocarbon gas combustion products employed for heating the catalyst 14.
The portion of the hydrocarbon gas combustion products remaining after the starting product formation is delivered, by-passes the catalyst 14, into the outlet pipe 12 for the removal of the combustion products. The evacuated combustion products are cooled in the cooler 31, their flow rate being controlled by the valve 33 and maintained constant by means of the flow rate controller 32 depending on the pressure variation in the system.
The process temperature during the controller atmosphere production is measured by means of the thermocouple 20 placed in the heating space 17.
In the operation of the apparatus shown in Figure 4, the catalyst 14 is additionally heated with at least a portion of the hydrocarbon gas combustion products remaining after the starting product formation, which is effected by delivering this portion of the hydrocarbon gas combustion products along the inner wall of the pipe 22 positioned inside the bucket 23.
The invention will be further described with reference to the following illustrative
Examples.
Example 1
The process was conducted in an apparatus for producing controlled atmospheres provided with a vessel filled with a nickel catalyst heated to a temperature of 1,0500C. The apparatus capacity was 60 m3/hr of the controlled atmosphere. A starting hydrocarbon gas having the composition 94.0% CW, 2.0%l C2H, 1.5% H, 2.5% N2 in admixture with air was supplied into a burner and burnt in a heating space at an air supply rate factor equal to 1.05.
50 vol.% of the combustion products were passed into the catalyst bed, into which the hydrocarbon gas was additionally fed at a volume ratio to the hydrocarbon gas combustion products of 30:70.
The starting product was passed through the catalyst and then cooled to a temperature of 300C to obtain a controlled atmosphere of the composition 20% CO, 40% H2 and 40% N2. The portion of the combustion products remaining after the starting product formation was removed from the apparatus bypassing the catalyst.
Example 2
The process was conducted in an apparatus comprising an annular vessel filled with a nickel catalyst. The process parameters were similar to those described in Example 1. A hydrocarbon gas in admixture with air was supplied into the burner and burnt in the heating space at an air supply rate factor equal to 1.05. The combustion products in an amount of 70 vol.% were fed into the catalyst bed, into which the hydrocarbon gas was additionally supplied at a volume ratio to the hydrocarbon gas combustion products of 30:70.
The starting product was passed through the catalyst and then cooled to a temperature of 300C to give a controlled atmosphere of the composition 20% CO, 40% H2 and 40% N 30 vol.% of the hydrocarbon gas combustion products remaining after the starting product formation were passed along the internal surface of the catalyst vessel to provide for additional heating thereof.
Example 3
The apparatus for carrying out the process and process parameters were the same as described in Example 1. A hydrocarbon gas in admixture with air was supplied into the burner and burnt in the heating space at an air supply rate factor equal to 0.6. The combustion products in an amount of 100 vol.% were fed into the catalyst bed, into which the hydrocarbon gas was additionally supplied at a volume ratio to the hydrocarbon gas combustion products of 6.6: 93.4.
The starting product was passed through the catalyst and then cooled to a temperature of 30"C to obtain a controlled atmosphere of the composition 3.5% COn 15% CO, 27% H2, 2.3% HO and 52.2% N2.
Example 4
The apparatus for carrying out the process and process parameters were the same as described in Example 1. A hydrocarbon gas mixed with air was supplied into the burner and burnt in the heating space at an air supply rate factor equal to 1.05. The combustion products in an amount of 100 vol.% were passed into the catalyst bed, into which the hydrocarbon gas was additionally fed at a volume ratio to the combustion products of 15:85.
The starting product was passed through the catalyst and then cooled to a temperature of 30"C to obtain a controlled atmosphere of the composition 3.5 % CO2, 15 %' CO, 27% H, 2.3% H2O and 52.2% N2.
Example 5
The apparatus for carrying out the process and the process parameters were the same as described in Example 1. A hydrocarbon gas mixed with air was supplied into the burner and burnt in the heating space at an air supply rate factor equal to 0.8. The combustion products in an amount of 100 vol.% were passed into the catalyst bed, into which the hydrocarbon gas was additionally fed at a volume ratio of 4: 96 to the hydrocarbon gas combustion products.
The starting product was passed through the catalyst and then cooled to a temperature of 300C to obtain a controlled atmosphere of the composition 5% CO2, 12%. CO, 18%
H2, 2.3% H20 and 62.7% N2.
Example 6
The apparatus for carrying out the process and the process parameters were the same as described in Example 1. A hydrocarbon gas mixed with air was supplied into the burner and burnt in the heating space at an air supply rate factor equal to 1.05. The combustion products in an amount of 100 vol.% were passed into the catalyst bed, in which the hydrocarbon gas was additionally fed at a volume ratio to the hydrocarbon gas combustion products of 1:99.
The starting product was passed through the catalyst and then cooled at a temperature of 300C to obtain a controlled atmosphere of the composition 10.5% CO2, 2.0% CO, 2.0% H2, 2.3% H20 and 83.2% N2.
The above Examples 1, 3, 4, 5 and 6 illustrating the process according to the present invention may be carried out in any embodiment of the apparatus shown in
Figures 1, 2, 3, 5 and 6. Example 2 also illustrating the process according to the present invention may be carried out in the apparatus shown in Figure 4.
Therefore, the process and apparatus according to the present invention ensure the creation of controlled atmospheres within a wide range of compositions thereof corresponding to combustion of the hydrocarbon gas at an air supply rate factor of from 0.25 to 0.95, including the compositions obtained upon combustion of the hydrocarbon gas at an air supply rate factor of from 0.4 to 0.6. The quality of the controlled atmospheres produced by the process according to the present invention is substantially improved; thus, the content of oxygen in the controlled atmosphere is reduced to 0.001% and even less.
The apparatus according to the present invention offers a considerable economic efficiency due to the replacement of two apparatuses with a single one, wherein the formation of two (endo- and exo-) controlled atmospheres is possible.
Production of controlled atmospheres with compositions 3.5-5%! CO2, 10-17% CO, 1227% H2, 2.3% H2O, balance N2 in the apparatus according to the present invention makes it possible to reduce the hydrocarbon gas consumption by 20 to 30%, as compared to the production of an endothermal > - con- trolled atmosphere.
WHAT WE CLAIM IS:
1. A process for producing controlled atmospheres by supplying a starting product onto a catalyst externally heated by the combustion products of a hydrocarbon gas, the starting product being a mixture of an additionally fed hydrocarbon gas and at least a portion of the combustion products of the said hydrocarbon gas employed for heating the catalyst.
2. A process as claimed in Claim 1, wherein the mixture of the additionally fed hydrocarbon gas and at least a portion of the combustion products of the hydrocarbon gas employed for the formation of the starting product is taken in a volume ratio between the components of
hydrocarbon gas 1-30
combustion products 99--70
3. A process as claimed in Claim 1 or 2, wherein the combustion products of the hydrocarbon gas employed for the catalyst heating are obtained at an air supply rate factor of from 0.6 to 1.05.
4. A process as claimed in any of Claims 1 to 3, wherein the portion of the combustion products of the hydrocarbon gas employed for the formation of the starting product constitutes 50 to 100 vol.% of the total amount of the combustion products of the hydrocarbon gas employed for the catalyst heating.
5. Apparatus for producing controlled atmospheres, comprising an airtight casing accommodating at least one vessel filled with a catalyst onto which the starting product is in use supplied, the casing having an outlet pipe for the obtained controlled atmosphere and at least one wall-mounted burner for combustion of a hydrocarbon gas and further having respective pipes for the removal of the hydrocarbon gas combustion products and for the supply of a hydrocarbon gas onto the catalyst, the end portion of the said pipe for the supply of the hydrocarbon gas being positioned in the vessel filled with the catalyst, and the vessel filled with the catalyst being in the form of a bucket with a perforated bottom, the bucket communicating with the said outlet pipe for the obtained controlled atmosphere and having its open end extending into the heating space of the casing.
6. Apparatus as claimed in Claim 5, wherein the end portion of the said pipe for the supplying of the hydrocarbon gas onto the catalyst is inserted into the catalyst bed.
7. Apparatus as claimed in Claim 5 or

WHAT WE CLAIM IS:
1. A process for producing controlled atmospheres by supplying a starting product onto a catalyst externally heated by the combustion products of a hydrocarbon gas, the starting product being a mixture of an additionally fed hydrocarbon gas and at least a portion of the combustion products of the said hydrocarbon gas employed for heating the catalyst.
2. A process as claimed in Claim 1, wherein the mixture of the additionally fed hydrocarbon gas and at least a portion of the combustion products of the hydrocarbon gas employed for the formation of the starting product is taken in a volume ratio between the components of
hydrocarbon gas 1-30
combustion products 99--70
3. A process as claimed in Claim 1 or 2, wherein the combustion products of the hydrocarbon gas employed for the catalyst heating are obtained at an air supply rate factor of from 0.6 to 1.05.
4. A process as claimed in any of Claims 1 to 3, wherein the portion of the combustion products of the hydrocarbon gas employed for the formation of the starting product constitutes 50 to 100 vol.% of the total amount of the combustion products of the hydrocarbon gas employed for the catalyst heating.
5. Apparatus for producing controlled atmospheres, comprising an airtight casing accommodating at least one vessel filled with a catalyst onto which the starting product is in use supplied, the casing having an outlet pipe for the obtained controlled atmosphere and at least one wall-mounted burner for combustion of a hydrocarbon gas and further having respective pipes for the removal of the hydrocarbon gas combustion products and for the supply of a hydrocarbon gas onto the catalyst, the end portion of the said pipe for the supply of the hydrocarbon gas being positioned in the vessel filled with the catalyst, and the vessel filled with the catalyst being in the form of a bucket with a perforated bottom, the bucket communicating with the said outlet pipe for the obtained controlled atmosphere and having its open end extending into the heating space of the casing.
6. Apparatus as claimed in Claim 5, wherein the end portion of the said pipe for the supplying of the hydrocarbon gas onto the catalyst is inserted into the catalyst bed.
7. Apparatus as claimed in Claim 5 or
6, wherein the said pipe for the supply of the hydrocarbon gas onto the catalyst is disposed coaxially with the vessel filled with the catalyst.
8. Apparatus as claimed in any of Claims 5 to 7, wherein the said pipe for the removal of the hydrocarbon gas combustion products is located inside the vessel filled with the catalyst and coaxially therewith.
9. Apparatus as claimed in any of Claims 5 to 8, further comprising a cooler for cooling the removed combustion products of the hydrocarbon gas, the cooler being connected by means of a piping with the pipe for the removal of the combustion products.
10. Apparatus as claimed in Claim 9, further comprising a flow-rate controller for the removed combustion products of the hydrocarbon gas, the controller being connected by means of a piping with the cooler for cooling the removed combustion products.
11. Apparatus as claimed in any of Claims 5 to 10, further comprising a controller for adjusting the flow rate of the hydrocarbon gas supplied onto the catalyst depending on the composition of the obtained controlled atmosphere, the said controller being connected by means of a piping with the pipe for the supply of the hydrocarbon gas onto the catalyst.
12. A process for producing controlled atmospheres substantially as herein described with reference to Figure 1 of the accompanying drawings.
13. A process for producing controlled atmospheres substantially as herein described with reference to Figure 2 of the accompanying drawings.
14. A process for producing controlled atmospheres substantially as herein described with reference to Figure 3 of the accompanying drawings.
15. A process for producing controlled atmospheres substantially as herein described with reference to Figure 4 of the accompanying drawings.
16. A process for producing controlled atmospheres substantially as herein described with reference to Figure 5 of the accompanying drawings.
17. A process for producing controlled atmospheres substantially as herein described with reference to Figure 6 of the accompanying drawings.
18. A process for producing controlled atmospheres substantially as herein described in any of the foregoing Examples.
19. Apparatus for producing controlled atmospheres substantially as herein described with reference to, and as shown in, Figure 1 of the accompanying drawings.
20. Apparatus for producing controlled atmospheres substantially as herein described with reference to, and as shown in, Figure 2 of the accompanying drawings.
21. Apparatus for producing controlled atmospheres substantially as herein described with reference to, and as shown in, Figure 3 of the accompanying drawing.
22. Apparatus for producing controlled atmospheres substantially as herein described with reference to, and as shown in, Figure 4 of the accompanying drawing.
23. Apparatus for producing contrplled atmospheres substantially as herein described with reference to, and as shown in, Figure 5 of the accompanying drawing.
24. Apparatus for producing controlled atmospheres substantially as herein described with reference to, and as shown in, Figure 6 of the accompanying drawings.
